Swiss CitiesSteckborn

Steckborn

TGThurgau

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The abbot's long journey

Abbot Diethelm sets out from the Untersee in the depths of winter 1312, carrying the hopes of little Steckborn in a leather satchel. A child from the town secretly follows the abbot across the Alps to the emperor's camp near Florence, witnessing the momentous day when Henry VII signs the charter that transforms their lakeside settlement into a true market town -- protected by imperial law and a penalty of 10 marks of gold.

Create This Story

In the winter of 1312/1313, Reichenau Abbot Diethelm von Castell traveled from the Untersee all the way to Emperor Henry VII's military camp near Florence to petition for market privileges for Steckborn. On January 26, 1313, the emperor issued a charter granting Steckborn the right to hold a weekly market every Thursday. The original charter document is preserved in the cantonal archive in Frauenfeld. Merchants traveling to Steckborn's market received imperial protection, with a penalty of 10 marks of gold for anyone hindering the town's market privileges. The charter gave Steckborn the three defining features of a medieval city: weekly market, city rights, and fortification walls.

The pile-dweller's secret

A child swimming in the Untersee discovers an ancient wooden post sticking out of the lake bed. Touching it transports them 5,000 years back to a Bronze Age stilt village, where they must help build a new platform before the autumn floods arrive. Among pile-dwellers who craft the oldest wheels and textiles in Europe, the child learns that the lake has always been both home and highway.

Create This Story

The shallow shores of the Untersee around Steckborn were home to Neolithic and Bronze Age pile-dwelling settlements built on wooden platforms raised above the water on stilts, dating from roughly 5000 to 500 BC. These are among the 111 sites inscribed as the UNESCO World Heritage Site 'Prehistoric Pile Dwellings around the Alps' (designated June 27, 2011). The oxygen-poor lake-bed conditions preserved organic materials: food remains, wooden tools, textiles, weapons, and jewelry thousands of years old. Discoveries from Lake Constance sites include some of the oldest wooden wheels in the world (around 3,400 BC) and the oldest textiles in Europe (around 3,000 BC).

The lace-maker's pattern

A mysterious Kloeppelbrief (lace pattern) found in the attic of the Haus zur Glocke turns out to be a map of the old town, with each knot marking a hidden treasure buried before the great fire of 1668. A child must learn the ancient art of bobbin lace-making to decode the pattern, following its delicate threads through the half-timbered streets of Steckborn to uncover what the lace-makers concealed three centuries ago.

Create This Story

For roughly 100 years, Steckborn was a center of bobbin lace-making (Kloeppelei), a delicate textile craft using multiple threads wound onto spindle-shaped bobbins, crossed in pairs and twisted together. Lace traders operated from the Haus zur Glocke, a mercerie (notions shop) in the old town, bringing raw materials and patterns called 'Kloeppelbriefe' to home workers who needed supplementary income. The finished lace was sold onward, with Steckborn's lace-makers collaborating with the hat industry in Wohlen (AG). The craft declined with industrialization but is preserved in the Turmhof museum's collection. The great fire of 1668 destroyed large parts of the medieval town center.

Gegauf's last machine

On the night of the 1895 Feldbach fire, young Karl Friedrich Gegauf rushes into the burning convent where his workshop stands. Flames consume the ancient monastery, but Gegauf has only one thought: to save his hemstitch prototype, the only machine of its kind in the world. Guided through the smoke by its rhythmic clicking, he rescues the invention that will one day become Bernina -- a global brand born from the ashes of a medieval nunnery.

Create This Story

In 1890, Karl Friedrich Gegauf (1860-1926) moved to Steckborn and opened a workshop for his invention: a monogram embroidery machine. On February 27, 1892, the Patent Office granted him patent No. 4670 for the world's first hemstitch sewing machine, capable of 100 stitches per minute. He set up production in the old Feldbach Convent buildings. By 1900, the workshop employed 70-80 people. In 1895, a fire destroyed the main building and church of Feldbach Monastery -- Gegauf's sewing machine prototype was famously the only thing rescued from the workshop. After his death in 1926, his sons Fritz and Gustav developed the first Bernina household sewing machine, entering production by end of 1932.

The tower's six thousand years

A child visiting the Turmhof museum at night finds that each floor comes alive with a different age: the ground floor fills with lake water and pile dwellers building their stilt homes, the first floor echoes with Roman soldiers marching along the Untersee, and the top floor glows with the forge of a medieval knight. The child must climb through 6,000 years of history to find the way back to the present before the museum opens at dawn.

Create This Story

The Turmhof (tower house) is Steckborn's most iconic building, a fortified residential tower built around 1282 by Reichenau Abbot Albrecht von Ramstein. It originally served as the seat of the Reichenau abbots when visiting their mainland possessions. A coat-of-arms tower was added in 1602. The building passed to the city of Steckborn in 1639, served as a school from 1856 to 1864, and has housed a local history museum since 1937. The Museum im Turmhof spans three floors covering 6,000 years of history -- from Neolithic pile dwellers to Romans, Alemanni, and modern times. It features stove building, bobbin lace-making, and a Bernina sewing machine collection.

The watchman of the Pulverturm

The last watchman of the medieval Watch and Powder Tower refuses to leave his post even as the town modernizes around him. A child befriends the old guardian and learns what he has been watching for all these centuries -- not invaders from across the Untersee, but the quiet, everyday moments that make a small lakeside town worth protecting: fishermen at dawn, the Thursday market, and the lights flickering on in half-timbered houses at dusk.

Create This Story

The Wacht- und Pulverturm (Watch and Powder Tower) is a medieval defensive tower on the northwestern Seepromenade, first mentioned in 1451, with a foundation of large glacial boulders. It was restored in 1907. The tower served a dual purpose: as a watchtower scanning the Untersee for approaching threats and as a powder magazine storing gunpowder and munitions. The Seepromenade connects the old town's landmarks with views across the Untersee to Germany's Hoeri peninsula. Steckborn's medieval town plan forms an isosceles triangle with the parish church at the apex and the lakefront as the base.

Nuns on the lake

Abbess Sophia and her 20 Cistercian sisters arrive at windswept Feldbach in 1253 to find only a ruined castle on a lake peninsula. A child from the village helps the nuns build their new home stone by stone, discovering that even the strictest monastic rules cannot silence laughter, and that a community of determined women can turn a crumbling fortress into a place of learning, justice, and prayer that endures for six centuries.

Create This Story

Kloster Feldbach was a Cistercian nunnery founded on a small peninsula just east of Steckborn's old town. In 1252, nobleman Kuno von Feldbach assigned his castle with fields and vineyard to a sister association from Constance. In 1253, Abbess Sophia moved with 20 nuns to Feldbach, where they adopted the strict Cistercian rule. The abbesses proved clever administrators, building up their own judicial authority over surrounding places. An early Gothic monastery church was likely built around 1327. The monastery was abolished in 1848 by the Thurgau Grand Council; in 1853, the remaining nuns (5 choir women and 4 lay sisters) moved to Gwiggen in Vorarlberg. Today the site is the See & Park Hotel Feldbach.

The sewing machine that stitched the world together

A magical Bernina sewing machine in the Steckborn factory can stitch any two places in the world together with a single thread. When a child accidentally sews Steckborn to a faraway city, the lakeside town and the distant metropolis merge in wonderful confusion -- fishermen on the Untersee find themselves next to skyscrapers, and the child must find the right thread to unpick the seam and bring everyone safely home to the little town that built a world-famous machine.

Create This Story

After Karl Friedrich Gegauf's death in 1926, his sons Fritz (1893-1980) and Gustav developed the first household sewing machine, naming it 'Bernina' after the Bernina Pass. It entered production by the end of 1932. In 1938, the first Bernina zigzag machine was introduced; in 1945, the world's first portable zigzag sewing machine with a free arm was launched. By mid-1963, one million Bernina zigzag machines had been manufactured in Steckborn. Today, Bernina still maintains its headquarters and main factory in Steckborn -- a remarkable example of a global brand rooted in a small Swiss town of fewer than 4,000 residents.
